article.title: A forecast of the fair market value of SpaceX's businesses

summary: SpaceX confidentially filed for a June 2026 IPO targeting a $1.75 trillion valuation, potentially the largest ever. A detailed segment-by-segment forecast suggests this price assumes exceptional outperformance across all business units, with significant upside risk and reliance on intangible synergies.
keyPoints: IPO filing date: April 1, 2026; targeted valuation: $1.75 trillion; planned listing: June 2026.
Seven business segments forecasted to total $1.25 trillion at median values, 29% below IPO target.
Starlink (consumer, enterprise, direct-to-cell) accounts for $602 billion or 48% of segment value.
xAI valued at $258 billion despite $1.46 billion quarterly losses and $430 million revenue.
Starship valued at $170 billion as an option on future technology; physical assets worth ~$46 billion.
75th percentile forecasts across segments approach $1.675 trillion, indicating IPO pricing assumes 'everything goes right'.

rewrite.title: SpaceX IPO Valuation Forecast: $1.75T Target in 2026
rewrite.body:
SpaceX has confidentially filed for an initial public offering planned for June 2026, aiming for a historic $1.75 trillion valuation. This figure would eclipse all previous IPOs, reflecting the company’s evolution into a multifaceted conglomerate spanning satellite broadband, space launch, AI, and defense sectors. A granular forecast dividing SpaceX into seven core business segments reveals a median fair market value of approximately $1.25 trillion, significantly below the IPO target. This gap highlights the market’s expectation that each segment will substantially outperform current projections over the next two years.

Starlink dominates the valuation, contributing nearly half of the segment value at $602 billion, driven by an anticipated subscriber base expansion from 9.2 million to over 50 million and diversified revenue streams including enterprise and direct-to-cell services. However, skepticism remains about the feasibility of such rapid growth and monetization. The AI unit, xAI, is valued at $258 billion despite ongoing quarterly losses exceeding $1.4 billion, relying heavily on optimistic merger assumptions rather than current financial performance.

Starship’s $170 billion valuation reflects option value on its still-developing technology rather than existing revenue, while tangible assets like satellites and launch infrastructure are valued at a modest $46 billion. The overall IPO pricing appears to price in a bullish scenario where all segments succeed simultaneously, supported by a conglomerate premium driven by unique synergies between space infrastructure and AI capabilities.

This ambitious valuation also anticipates strong retail demand, with a reported 30% retail allocation, well above typical IPO levels. While intangible factors and market enthusiasm could justify a premium, the forecast underscores that realizing the $1.75 trillion valuation hinges on exceptional execution and growth across all fronts. Investors should weigh the high-risk, high-reward nature of this landmark offering carefully.
rewrite.disclaimer: AI-assisted rewrite based on extracted text + discussion signals
